source_text:
Voyager 1 launched from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977, sixteen days after its sister probe Voyager 2 — a counterintuitive ordering explained by the different trajectories NASA had chosen for the two spacecraft. Riding a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, the probe began a primary mission of five years: a planetary flyby of Jupiter and Saturn during a rare alignment of the outer planets that occurs roughly once every 176 years.

It reached Jupiter on March 5, 1979, sending back the first detailed images of the planet's faint ring system and discovering active volcanoes on its moon Io — the first observation of active volcanism beyond Earth. Twenty months later, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 made its closest approach to Saturn, photographing the ring structure in unprecedented detail and capturing close-ups of the moon Titan. The Titan trajectory pushed Voyager 1 above the plane of the solar system, ending its planetary tour but setting it on a path toward interstellar space.

On February 14, 1990, at the urging of astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 turned its camera back toward home and photographed Earth from roughly 6 billion kilometers away. The image — a single pixel against the sun's scattered light — became known as the Pale Blue Dot. It was the spacecraft's final photograph; the cameras were powered down afterwards to conserve energy.

Voyager 1 crossed the heliopause on August 25, 2012, becoming the first human-made object to enter interstellar space. It carries the Golden Record, a 12-inch gold-plated copper phonograph disc curated by a team led by Sagan, containing greetings in 55 languages, music, and images intended for any intelligence that might one day find it. Power comes from three radioisotope thermoelectric generators fueled by plutonium-238, whose 87.7-year half-life has slowly drained the spacecraft's energy budget.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is roughly 25 billion kilometers from Earth, traveling at about 61,000 kilometers per hour. In November 2026, it will become the first probe to cross one light-day of distance.

rounds.output_text:
Launched from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977, Voyager 1 followed its sister probe, Voyager 2, sixteen days later. This seemingly backward sequencing was due to the distinct paths NASA had planned for the two spacecraft. Propelled by a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, the initial five-year primary mission involved surveying the outer planets—Jupiter and Saturn—during a seldom-occurring alignment of these giants, an event happening approximately every 176 years.

The probe arrived at Jupiter on March 5, 1979. There, it transmitted the first high-resolution pictures of the planet's subtle ring system and documented active volcanoes on its moon Io, marking the initial observation of volcanism beyond Earth. Twenty months thereafter, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 approached Saturn most closely, vividly documenting its ring system and capturing detailed close-ups of Titan. The journey toward Titan elevated Voyager 1 above the plane of our solar system, concluding its planetary circuit while simultaneously setting it on a course into interstellar space.

Following the suggestion of astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 aimed its instruments back toward Earth on February 14, 1990, capturing a picture of our planet from about 6 billion kilometers distant. This image, appearing as just one pixel against the Sun's diffused glow, became famously known as the Pale Blue Dot. This represented the spacecraft's last photograph; subsequently, the cameras were shut down to preserve power.

Voyager 1 transitioned into interstellar space on August 25, 2012, achieving the distinction of being the first human artifact to enter that region. It bears the Golden Record, a 12-inch copper phonograph disc covered in gold. This disc, assembled under the guidance of a team led by Sagan, contains greetings in 55 languages, musical selections, and imagery intended for any intelligence that might eventually encounter it. Powering its journey are three radioisotope thermoelectric generators utilizing plutonium-238, whose 87.7-year half-life has gradually depleted the spacecraft's power reserves.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is situated roughly 25 billion kilometers from Earth, maintaining a speed of about 61,000 kilometers per hour. Furthermore, in November 2026, it will reach the milestone of crossing one light-day of distance.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is about 25 billion kilometers away from Earth, maintaining a velocity of around 61,000 kilometers per hour. Furthermore, in November 2026, it will reach the significant milestone of having traveled one light-day.

Launched from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977, Voyager 1 was followed sixteen days later by its counterpart, Voyager 2. This staggered deployment occurred because NASA had planned different paths for the two spacecraft. The mission, utilizing a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, had a primary five-year goal: to observe the outer planets, Jupiter and Saturn, during a rare celestial alignment of these gigantic bodies, an event occurring approximately every 176 years.

The probe reached Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this encounter, it transmitted the first high-resolution pictures of the planet's subtle ring system and documented vigorous volcanism on its moon Io, marking the first observation of such activity originating outside of Earth. Two years later, specifically on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 made its closest approach to Saturn, offering vibrant documentation of its rings and capturing detailed close-ups of Titan. The transit near Titan caused Voyager 1 to depart the solar system's plane, concluding its planetary tour while simultaneously beginning its trajectory into interstellar space.

Following a suggestion from astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 aimed its instruments back toward Earth on February 14, 1990, capturing an image of our planet from a distance nearing 6 billion kilometers. This photograph, appearing as a solitary pixel against the Sun's diffused brightness, became famously known as the Pale Blue Dot. This marks the final image taken by the spacecraft; subsequently, the cameras were powered down to conserve energy.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, achieving the status of the initial human-made object to reach that region. It carries the Golden Record, a 12-inch copper phonograph disc covered in gold. This disc, compiled with input from a team including Sagan, contains greetings in 55 languages, musical selections, and visuals intended for any intelligence that might eventually discover it. The spacecraft's operation is sustained by three radioisotope thermoelectric generators using plutonium-238, whose 87.7-year half-life has progressively diminished the power supply.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is approximately 25 billion kilometers distant from Earth, maintaining a speed near 61,000 kilometers per hour. Additionally, in November 2026, it will achieve the notable milestone of having traveled one light-day.

Launched from the site at Cape Canaveral, Voyager 1 began its mission on September 5, 1977. Voyager 2 followed sixteen days later, as NASA had allocated distinct objectives to the two probes. The undertaking, which was launched aboard a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, had a central goal that spanned five years: to observe the outer planets, namely Jupiter and Saturn, capitalizing on a rare astronomical alignment of these massive bodies, an occurrence that happens roughly every 176 years.

The probe reached Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this encounter, it transmitted the initial high-resolution imagery of the planet’s faint ring structure and documented the intense volcanism on its moon Io, which represented the first recorded instances of such activity originating beyond Earth. Two years following this, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 executed its closest approach to Saturn, successfully recording clear views of its rings and gathering detailed views of Titan. This close flyby of Titan caused Voyager 1 to depart the ecliptic plane of the solar system, thereby concluding its planetary tour while simultaneously beginning its journey into interstellar space.

At the suggestion of astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 redirected its instruments toward Earth on February 14, 1990, capturing an image of our planet from a distance approaching 6 billion kilometers. This photograph, appearing as a single pixel against the diffused brightness of the Sun, became famously known as the Pale Blue Dot. This marked the final photograph taken by the spacecraft; after this, the cameras were powered down to conserve energy.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, becoming the first human-made object to achieve this status. It carries the Golden Record, a 12-inch, gold-plated copper phonograph disc. This disc, developed with input including Sagan's, contains greetings in 55 languages, selected musical compositions, and visuals intended for any civilization that might eventually discover it. The spacecraft remains powered by three radioisotope thermoelectric generators utilizing plutonium-238, whose power supply diminishes due to its 87.7-year half-life.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is located approximately 25 billion kilometers from Earth, sustaining a velocity near 61,000 kilometers per hour. Moreover, in November 2026, it will reach the notable benchmark of having traveled one light-day.

rounds.output_text:
Launched from the facility located at Cape Canaveral, the Voyager 1 probe embarked on its mission on September 5, 1977. Voyager 2 launched sixteen days later, as NASA had assigned distinct objectives to each of the two probes. The mission, which departed aboard a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, had a primary five-year goal: to observe the outer planets, namely Jupiter and Saturn. This was made possible by a fortuitous celestial alignment of these massive bodies, an occurrence that happens roughly every 176 years.

The probe reached Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this close pass, it transmitted the first high-resolution images showing the planet’s faint ring system and documented the intense volcanism on its moon Io, providing the first recorded evidence of such activity originating beyond Earth. Two years later, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 conducted its closest flyby of Saturn, successfully obtaining clear visuals of its rings and collecting extensive data regarding Titan. This near encounter with Titan caused Voyager 1 to depart from the ecliptic plane of our solar system, thus concluding its planetary tour while simultaneously beginning its voyage into interstellar space.

Following a suggestion from astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 oriented its instruments back toward Earth on February 14, 1990, capturing an image of our planet from a distance approaching 6 billion kilometers. This particular photograph, which appeared as a solitary pixel against the diffused glare of the Sun, became famously known as the Pale Blue Dot. This represented the final image taken by the spacecraft; afterward, the cameras were deactivated to conserve power.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, becoming the first human-made object to achieve this status. It carries the Golden Record, a copper phonograph disc plated in gold that measures 12 inches. This disc, developed with input including Sagan’s, contains greetings in 55 languages, selected musical pieces, and visuals intended for any future civilization that might encounter it. The spacecraft sustains itself using three radioisotope thermoelectric generators powered by plutonium-238, whose energy source degrades due to its 87.7-year half-life.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is situated approximately 25 billion kilometers away from Earth, maintaining a velocity approaching 61,000 kilometers per hour. Moreover, in November 2026, it will reach the important milestone of having traveled one light-day.
